title: The signpainter who gave a city one alphabet
summary: Explain how a single signpainter's hand-lettered alphabet quietly spread across an entire city's shopfronts, and why a place can have a 'voice' you only hear when it disappears, using past tenses, present perfect, and explanation.
topic: How can one craftsman's lettering become the invisible voice of a whole city?
prompt: Write a 3-paragraph explanatory essay (400–500 words) about how Senhor Reis, a signpainter in a Portuguese town, hand-painted shop signs for forty years, and how his particular letterforms slowly became the 'look' of the whole high street without anyone planning it. Explain how this happened, then explain why people only noticed the town had a visual voice once plastic signs began to replace his work. Explain a process and a cause; do not argue a side. Use the past simple, the present perfect, and 'because' or 'this is why' at least once.
hintStarters: Nobody hired Senhor Reis to design the look of the town; it happened slowly because…
When a new shop opened, the owner usually asked for a sign that matched the others, and this is why…
People rarely notice a visual voice while it is still speaking; they only hear it once…

hintVocab.meaningVi: chữ viết tay được sơn vẽ
mà không ai lên kế hoạch
đặc tính của một con phố
một ngôn ngữ thị giác chung
một tiêu chuẩn không nói thành lời

sampleText:
Nobody hired Senhor Reis to design the look of the town of Estremoz. He was simply the only signpainter for thirty kilometres, and for forty years, whenever a shop needed a name above its door, its owner walked up the hill to his small workshop and asked him to paint one. He had a particular hand: rounded capitals, a slight forward lean, and a way of leaving a thin shadow on the right side of every letter, as if the words were standing in afternoon sun. He did not think of this as a style. It was simply how his wrist moved. But because he painted almost every sign in town, that wrist quietly became the handwriting of the whole high street.

The look spread the way a habit spreads, not the way a plan does. When a new shop opened, the owner usually asked for a sign that matched the others, because a sign that clashed with its neighbours looked like a stranger who had wandered into a family photograph. Reis was happy to oblige, and so his rounded capitals and afternoon shadows appeared above the bakery, the pharmacy, the shoe shop, and the bank, year after year. This is why visitors often said the street 'felt like one place' without being able to explain what they meant. They were reading a single hand. The town had a voice, and the voice was a man with a brush who never once described what he was doing.

People rarely notice a visual voice while it is still speaking. They only hear it once it stops. Over the past fifteen years, plastic and backlit signs have arrived, each one designed in a different city by a different machine, each one perfectly clear and perfectly anonymous. The first few blended in. Then, slowly, the painted signs began to disappear, repainted or replaced when a shop changed hands, and the street started to sound like a room where everyone is talking in a different accent. Older residents have begun to say the high street 'doesn't feel like itself' anymore, and they are right, though most cannot name the reason. The reason is that they have lost a shared hand. Senhor Reis is old now and no longer paints. What he made was never a single famous sign; it was the quiet agreement, held across forty years, that the whole street would speak in the same gentle letters. That agreement has ended, and a town only learns it had such a thing on the day it is gone.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cause with 'because' and 'this is why'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: '…because he painted almost every sign in town' nêu nguyên nhân, còn 'This is why visitors often said the street felt like one place' nêu kết quả. 'Because' đứng trước nguyên nhân; 'this is why' đứng trước kết quả.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: '…because he painted almost every sign in town' gives the cause, while 'This is why visitors often said the street felt like one place' gives the result. 'Because' introduces a cause; 'this is why' introduces a result.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for recent change with present relevance
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Over the past fifteen years, plastic and backlit signs have arrived' và 'Older residents have begun to say…' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để mô tả những thay đổi gần đây mà hậu quả vẫn còn cảm nhận được ở hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Over the past fifteen years, plastic and backlit signs have arrived' and 'Older residents have begun to say…' use the present perfect to describe recent changes whose effects are still felt now.

sampleTranslationVi:
Không ai thuê Senhor Reis thiết kế diện mạo của thị trấn Estremoz. Ông chỉ đơn giản là người vẽ biển hiệu duy nhất trong vòng ba mươi cây số, và suốt bốn mươi năm, mỗi khi một cửa hàng cần một cái tên phía trên cửa, chủ của nó đi lên dốc đến xưởng nhỏ của ông và nhờ ông vẽ một cái. Ông có một nét tay riêng: những chữ in hoa tròn trịa, hơi nghiêng về phía trước, và một cách để lại một bóng mỏng ở phía bên phải mỗi chữ cái, như thể những chữ đang đứng trong nắng chiều. Ông không nghĩ đây là một phong cách. Đó chỉ đơn giản là cách cổ tay ông chuyển động. Nhưng vì ông vẽ gần như mọi biển hiệu trong thị trấn, cổ tay đó lặng lẽ trở thành nét chữ của cả con phố chính.

Diện mạo lan ra theo cách một thói quen lan ra, không phải cách một kế hoạch lan ra. Khi một cửa hàng mới mở, chủ thường nhờ một biển hiệu khớp với những cái khác, bởi vì một biển hiệu xung đột với những cái bên cạnh trông như một người lạ lạc vào một bức ảnh gia đình. Reis vui lòng chiều theo, và thế là những chữ in hoa tròn trịa và bóng nắng chiều của ông xuất hiện phía trên tiệm bánh, hiệu thuốc, cửa hàng giày, và ngân hàng, năm này qua năm khác. Đây là lý do du khách thường nói con phố 'cảm thấy như một nơi duy nhất' mà không thể giải thích họ muốn nói gì. Họ đang đọc một nét chữ duy nhất. Thị trấn có một giọng nói, và giọng nói đó là một người đàn ông cầm cọ chưa bao giờ một lần mô tả điều mình đang làm.

Mọi người hiếm khi nhận ra một giọng nói thị giác khi nó vẫn còn đang nói. Họ chỉ nghe thấy nó một khi nó ngừng lại. Trong mười lăm năm qua, các biển hiệu nhựa và đèn nền đã xuất hiện, mỗi cái được thiết kế ở một thành phố khác nhau bởi một cỗ máy khác nhau, mỗi cái hoàn toàn rõ ràng và hoàn toàn vô danh. Vài cái đầu tiên hòa vào. Rồi, từ từ, những biển hiệu sơn tay bắt đầu biến mất, bị sơn lại hoặc thay thế khi một cửa hàng đổi chủ, và con phố bắt đầu nghe như một căn phòng nơi mọi người đều nói bằng một giọng khác nhau. Những cư dân lớn tuổi đã bắt đầu nói con phố chính 'không còn cảm thấy là chính nó' nữa, và họ đúng, dù hầu hết không thể gọi tên lý do. Lý do là họ đã đánh mất một nét chữ chung. Senhor Reis giờ đã già và không còn vẽ nữa. Thứ ông tạo ra chưa bao giờ là một biển hiệu nổi tiếng đơn lẻ; đó là thỏa thuận thầm lặng, được giữ suốt bốn mươi năm, rằng cả con phố sẽ nói bằng cùng những chữ cái dịu dàng. Thỏa thuận đó đã kết thúc, và một thị trấn chỉ biết mình từng có một thứ như vậy vào ngày nó biến mất.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'because' correctly to give the cause?
options: Because of he painted almost every sign in town, his hand became the town's look.
Because he painted almost every sign in town, his hand became the town's look.
Because he painting almost every sign in town, his hand became the town's look.
Because that he painted almost every sign in town, his hand became the town's look.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Because + subject + verb' introduces a cause. Option B adds 'of' before a clause. Option C uses 'painting' without an auxiliary. Option D adds 'that' after 'because'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Because' is followed by a full clause; 'because of' is followed by a noun.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'this is why' correctly to give a result?
options: This is why visitors said the street felt like one place because.
This is the why visitors said the street felt like one place.
This is why visitors said the street felt like one place.
This is why that visitors said the street felt like one place.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'This is why + result clause' is correct. Option B adds a stray 'because'. Option C inserts 'the' before 'why'. Option D adds 'that' after 'why'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'This is why' is followed directly by the result clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly for recent arrivals?
options: Over the past fifteen years, plastic signs have arrive.
Over the past fifteen years, plastic signs arrived.
Over the past fifteen years, plastic signs has arrived.
Over the past fifteen years, plastic signs have arrived.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have arrived' is the present perfect with 'over the past', and it agrees with the plural subject. Option B uses base 'arrive'. Option C uses simple past, losing the link to now. Option D uses 'has' with a plural subject.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Over the past …' pairs with the present perfect; match the plural subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly for an earlier action?
options: A clashing sign looked like a stranger who had wandered into a photograph.
A clashing sign looked like a stranger who has wandered into a photograph.
A clashing sign looked like a stranger who had wander into a photograph.
A clashing sign looked like a stranger who wandered into a photograph had.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect 'had wandered' marks an action before the past 'looked'. Option B uses present perfect 'has', breaking the past sequence. Option C uses base 'wander'. Option D misplaces 'had' at the end.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect = had + past participle for the earlier of two past actions.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes the style spreading like a habit?
options: The look spread the way a habit spread, not the way a plan do.
The look spread the way a habit spreads, not the way a plan does.
The look spread like a habit spreads, not how a plan does.
The look spread the way that a habit spreading, not the way a plan does.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The way a habit spreads … the way a plan does' keeps parallel present-tense generalisations. Option B uses 'do' with 'a plan'. Option C mixes 'like' and 'how' awkwardly. Option D uses 'spreading' without an auxiliary.
mcQuestions.hint: Keep the two 'the way …' clauses parallel, with correct subject–verb agreement.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'as if' correctly with the past subjunctive?
options: He left a shadow, as if the words was standing in afternoon sun.
He left a shadow, as if the words are standing in afternoon sun.
He left a shadow, as if the words were standing in afternoon sun.
He left a shadow, as if the words be standing in afternoon sun.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'As if … were' uses the subjunctive for an imagined comparison. Option B uses 'was' (less standard after 'as if' for an unreal idea, and disagreeing in number). Option C uses present 'are'. Option D uses bare 'be'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'As if' often takes 'were' for an unreal, imagined comparison.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect with 'begin to' correctly?
options: Older residents have began to say the street doesn't feel like itself.
Older residents have begun saying to the street doesn't feel like itself.
Older residents have begun to saying the street doesn't feel like itself.
Older residents have begun to say the street doesn't feel like itself.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have begun to say' uses the participle 'begun' and 'to + base verb'. Option B uses 'began' after 'have'. Option C adds a stray 'to' and uses 'saying'. Option D uses 'to saying'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use the participle 'begun' after 'have', and 'to + base verb' after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes the new signs as clear but characterless?
options: Each one is perfectly clear and perfectly anonymous.
Each one is perfect clear and perfect anonymous.
Each one is perfectly clearly and perfectly anonymously.
Each one are perfectly clear and perfectly anonymous.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Perfectly' (adverb) modifies the adjectives 'clear' and 'anonymous'. Option B uses 'perfect' (adjective) to modify another adjective. Option C wrongly makes the describing words adverbs. Option D uses 'are' with the singular 'each one'.
mcQuestions.hint: An adverb ('perfectly') modifies an adjective; 'each one' is singular.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally expresses that people notice the voice only after it stops?
options: People rarely notice a visual voice while it is still speak.
People rarely notice a visual voice while it is still speaking.
People rarely notice a visual voice while it still speaking.
People rarely notices a visual voice while it is still speaking.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Is still speaking' is correct present continuous. Option B uses base 'speak'. Option C drops 'is'. Option D uses 'notices' with the plural 'people'.
mcQuestions.hint: Present continuous = is + -ing; 'people' takes a plural verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best states the writer's closing explanation?
options: A town only learns it had such a thing on the day it gone.
A town only learn it had such a thing on the day it is gone.
A town only learns it had such a thing on the day it is gone.
A town only learns it has such a thing on the day it is gone.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Learns … it had … it is gone' keeps consistent and natural tenses. Option B drops 'is' before 'gone'. Option C uses 'learn' without agreement. Option D uses present 'has', which clashes with the past sense of loss.
mcQuestions.hint: Match agreement on 'learns' and keep 'it is gone' complete.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'happy to' correctly to describe his willingness?
options: Reis was happy obliging.
Reis was happy to obliging.
Reis was happy that oblige.
Reis was happy to oblige.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Happy to + base verb' is the correct pattern. Option B uses 'obliging'. Option C uses 'to obliging'. Option D uses 'that oblige', which is ungrammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Happy to' is followed by the base verb.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The essay suggests a place can have a 'visual voice' that nobody designed and nobody notices until it disappears. Are there parts of the place you live that quietly share one look or sound? What might be lost if every shop, sign, or street started to look as though it came from somewhere else?
